text: Why consulting? And why specifically this firm?
Walk me through your resume.

material: McKinsey PEI Dimension: Personal Impact — The ability to influence others and create change without relying on positional authority.
text: Tell me about a time when you had to persuade a group of people to change their approach or perspective on an important issue.

material: McKinsey PEI Dimension: Entrepreneurial Drive — Building something from scratch or achieving results significantly beyond expectations.
text: Tell me about a time when you identified an opportunity and took initiative to create something of significant impact, without being asked.

material: McKinsey PEI Dimension: Inclusive Leadership — The ability to lead diverse teams, resolve conflicts, and bring out the best in others.
text: Tell me about a time when you led a team through a challenging situation where team members had conflicting views or motivations.
Tell me about your most significant leadership experience.
Describe a time when you had to solve a problem with an analytical approach. Walk me through your process.
Tell me about a time when you faced a setback and how you overcame it.
What is your greatest professional achievement and why?
Where do you see yourself in five to ten years?
Tell me about a time when you had to manage multiple stakeholders with competing interests.
Describe a situation where you had to drive results in a team where you were not the formal leader.

material: BCG values collaboration and diverse thinking. They look for candidates who can work effectively with clients and team members from different backgrounds.
text: Give me an example of a time you worked with someone whose working style was very different from yours. How did you adapt?
Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult decision with significant trade-offs. How did you approach it?
What is the biggest risk you have ever taken? What was the outcome?
Tell me about a time you received tough feedback. What did you do with it?

material: Bain looks for candidates with a 'Bainee' collaborative culture fit and a track record of driving results.
text: Tell me about a time when you exceeded expectations on a deliverable. What drove you to go further?
Why should we hire you over other candidates?
Tell me about a time you had to communicate a complex idea to a non-technical audience.
Describe a situation where you had to work under significant time pressure. How did you prioritize?

material: McKinsey PEI Dimension: Courageous Change — Challenging the status quo and driving meaningful change even when it is uncomfortable.
text: Tell me about a time when you challenged a prevailing assumption or pushed back on an established way of doing things.
Tell me about a time you had to persuade a group of people who were initially resistant to your idea.
Describe a situation where you had to build trust quickly with someone who was skeptical of you.
Tell me about a time you had to tailor your communication style to influence a specific audience.
Describe a time you identified an opportunity that others had overlooked and took initiative to pursue it.
Tell me about a project where you had to figure things out with very little guidance or structure.
Give me an example of when you set an ambitious goal for yourself and what you did to achieve it.
Tell me about a time you brought together people with conflicting viewpoints to reach a common goal.
Describe a situation where you actively sought out a perspective that was different from your own to improve an outcome.
Tell me about a time you helped someone on your team who was struggling, and what impact that had.
Describe a time you challenged the status quo or pushed back against a popular opinion because you believed it was wrong.
Tell me about a situation where you had to adapt quickly to a significant and unexpected change.
Give an example of when you proposed a fundamentally different approach to solving a problem, even though it was unpopular.
Tell me about a complex topic outside your area of expertise that you taught yourself. What drove your interest?
Describe a time you had to work closely with someone whose personality or approach was very different from yours, and how you made the partnership effective.
Tell me about a time you realized midway through a project that your approach was not going to deliver the desired outcome. What did you do?
Describe a situation where you had to balance what a client or stakeholder wanted with what you believed was the right thing to do.
Tell me about a time you inherited a failing project or initiative. How did you turn it around?
What is something you have invested significant personal time in mastering, and why does it matter to you?
Describe a time you had to give up personal credit or visibility for the benefit of the team.
Tell me about a time you had to manage competing priorities with limited resources. How did you decide what to focus on?
Describe a situation where you received incomplete or ambiguous information and still had to make a decision. Walk me through your process.
Tell me about a time you failed to meet a commitment you made. What happened and what did you learn?
Give me an example of when you used data or analysis to change someone's mind.
Describe a time you had to deliver bad news to a senior leader or important stakeholder. How did you handle it?
Tell me about a time you had to quickly get up to speed in an unfamiliar industry or subject area. What was your approach?
Describe a situation where you had to manage a conflict between two team members. What was your role and what was the outcome?
Tell me about a time you proactively identified a problem before anyone else noticed it. What did you do?
Give an example of a time you had to simplify a complex process or system. What was your approach and what was the result?
Describe a situation where you had to make a trade-off between quality and speed. How did you decide?
Tell me about a time you motivated a team during a particularly challenging or low-morale period.
Describe an experience where you had to work with a difficult or underperforming team member. What steps did you take?
Tell me about a time you had to persuade a senior person to change course on a decision they had already made.
Give an example of when you took ownership of something that was not formally your responsibility.
Describe a project where the scope changed significantly after it had already started. How did you handle the ambiguity?
Tell me about a time you built a relationship with someone that later proved critical to a project's success.
Describe a situation where you disagreed with your manager or supervisor. How did you handle it?
Tell me about a time you had to delegate effectively under pressure. What was the outcome?
Give me an example of when you had to make a decision without all the information you would have liked. What framework did you use?
Describe a time you helped transform an organization, team, or process. What was your specific contribution?
Tell me about an experience where cultural differences or diversity played a significant role in how you approached a problem.
Describe a time when you had to hold yourself or your team accountable to a very high standard despite external pressure to cut corners.
Tell me about a time you developed a strategic recommendation. How did you structure your thinking and what was the outcome?
Describe a time you went above and beyond to deliver exceptional service to an internal or external client.
Tell me about a time you had to prioritize long-term value over short-term gains. What were the trade-offs?
Describe a situation where you identified a creative or unconventional solution to a problem that a traditional approach would not solve.
Tell me about a time you had to onboard or mentor someone new. How did you ensure they were set up for success?
Give an example of a time you received feedback that surprised you. How did you respond and what changed?
Describe a situation where you had to navigate organizational politics to get something done.
Tell me about a time you synthesized information from multiple sources to form a recommendation. How did you ensure rigor?
Describe a time you had to stand firm on an ethical issue even when it was inconvenient or costly.
Tell me about a time you launched something from scratch, whether a project, initiative, club, or business.
Give an example of when you used structured problem-solving to break down a large, ambiguous challenge into manageable parts.
Describe a time when your initial hypothesis or assumption turned out to be wrong. How did you adjust?
Tell me about a situation where you had to coordinate across multiple teams or departments to deliver a result.
Describe a time when you had to say no to a request from someone important. How did you handle the conversation?
Tell me about a situation where you leveraged technology or a new tool to significantly improve efficiency or outcomes.
Tell me about a time you had to present a recommendation to a hostile or highly critical audience. How did you prepare and what happened?
Describe a time you persisted through repeated obstacles or failures to achieve a goal that mattered to you.
Tell me about a time you empowered someone else to take on a leadership role. What was the impact?
Describe a skill or capability you deliberately developed over the past year. What was your approach to learning?

material:
Case: Retail Turnaround

Your client is FreshMart, a mid-size grocery chain with 85 stores across the Midwest United States. Same-store sales have declined 15% over the past two years, while the overall grocery market grew 3% annually. Key facts:

Revenue: $2.4B (down from $2.8B two years ago)
Operating margin: 1.2% (industry average: 3.5%)
Customer traffic: down 20%
Average basket size: up 6%
Employee turnover: 85% annually (industry average: 60%)
Online grocery delivery: 2% of sales (competitor average: 12%)

The CEO has hired your firm to diagnose the root causes and develop a turnaround plan within 12 months.
text: How would you structure your analysis of FreshMart's decline? What are the most likely root causes, and what would you prioritise in a turnaround plan?

material:
Case: Market Entry

Your client is EduBright, a leading online education platform based in India with 15 million users. They offer K-12 tutoring, test preparation, and professional certification courses in English and Hindi. Annual revenue is $400M, growing 40% year-over-year, with a gross margin of 65%.

The CEO wants to enter the Southeast Asian market, starting with Indonesia and Vietnam. Key context:
- Indonesia has 270M people with a median age of 29; internet penetration is 77%
- Vietnam has 100M people; the online education market is fragmented with no dominant player
- Local competitors charge 60-70% less than EduBright's Indian pricing
- Content would need to be localised into Bahasa Indonesia and Vietnamese
- EduBright has $200M in cash and no debt
text: How would you evaluate whether EduBright should enter these markets? What market entry strategy would you recommend, and what are the key risks?

material:
Case: Profitability

Your client is SkyWay Airlines, a regional European carrier operating 45 aircraft on 120 routes. Despite record passenger numbers last year (18 million), the airline reported a net loss of €60M. Key data:

Revenue per available seat km (RASK): €0.058
Cost per available seat km (CASK): €0.065
Load factor: 82% (industry average: 85%)
Fuel costs: 32% of operating costs (hedged at $85/barrel through Q2 only)
Fleet age: average 14 years (maintenance costs rising 8% annually)
On-time performance: 68% (industry average: 80%)
Ancillary revenue per passenger: €8 (low-cost competitor average: €22)

The board wants to achieve breakeven within 18 months without reducing the route network.
text: Walk me through how you would diagnose the profitability gap. What are the biggest levers available to close the RASK-CASK gap, and how would you sequence your recommendations?

material:
Case: Digital Transformation

Your client is Meridian Insurance, a 90-year-old property and casualty insurer with $8B in annual premiums. The company processes 500,000 claims per year, with an average processing time of 14 days. Customer satisfaction (NPS) has fallen from +32 to +11 over three years.

Key pain points:
- 70% of claims still require manual review by adjusters
- The core policy administration system is a 25-year-old mainframe application
- InsurTech competitors offer instant quotes and 48-hour claims resolution
- IT spend is 4% of revenue (industry leaders spend 7-9%)
- A previous $200M IT modernisation project was abandoned two years ago after cost overruns

The new CEO has asked your firm to design a digital transformation roadmap.
text: How would you structure a digital transformation plan for Meridian? Given the failed prior initiative, how would you build confidence with the board and manage execution risk?

material:
Case: Pricing Strategy

Your client is AquaPure, a premium bottled water brand that sells 500ml bottles for $3.50 in the US market. They currently hold 4% market share in the $20B US bottled water market. Key context:

- Brand perception: consumers rank AquaPure #1 in taste tests but #5 in brand awareness
- Distribution: available in 40% of grocery stores and 15% of convenience stores
- Gross margin: 62% (vs. 45% for mainstream brands)
- A major retailer (representing 20% of sales) has threatened to delist AquaPure unless they reduce wholesale prices by 15%
- Two new premium competitors have entered the market in the past year, priced at $2.75 and $4.00
- Raw material costs (PET plastic, filtration) have increased 12% this year
text: How would you advise AquaPure on the retailer's demand? More broadly, how should they think about their pricing strategy given the competitive and cost dynamics?
